This pull request contained a bug report and several unrelated changes, one of which suggested an implementation that fixed the reported bug.

1. I merged the part of this pull request that makes the maximum number of retries a configuration option because it makes sense and required little effort to apply (it's just that no one requested it until now). This was included in pip-accel 0.24 (e2f51e105c9b7d13fe1a784361f57786009222e1).

2. I'm also confident that the bug you reported is now fixed, but I didn't use the implementation you suggested. Support for clean upgrades and `pip uninstall` was achieved with the following changes combined:

 - pip-accel 0.25 (0bf3c2202d23e36bd7eb2fa2d25bbaea2ef4fd3c) implemented initial support for tracking of installed files. The commit message explains in more detail why I didn't use the implementation you suggested in this pull request.
 - pip-accel 0.29 (6902027816b3ff81cc2f13b82db3f9d768b2ed31, published today) improves support for tracking of installed files by evaluating all `setup.py` scripts using setuptools (regardless of whether the `setup.py` script imports distutils or setuptools).

3. I didn't do anything with 60a60c9a5b9632c9b74988b9c5dfb99493f974a5. If you want me to change this please open a separate issue describing your problem and in what environment you encounter the problem. For what it's worth, I've never seen a colon being problematic in a UNIX file system, and looking through the Docker issue tracker I can only find bugs in the Docker command line application `docker` treating colons awkwardly.

The bug reported in this pull request is now fixed so I'm going to close this pull request. In the future if you want to contribute, please consider using separate feature branches and pull requests for unrelated issues. It makes it much easier for me to discern related vs. unrelated changes and issues.

AdaptiveLearningRateSchedulers is a custom scheduler implemented in Sockeye. It relies on having a single object which gets informed about validation metric scores. With distributed training the scheduler is pickled and sent remotely. On the remote host no one would actually inform the scheduler about new evaluation metrics. So for distributed training it's best to simply pick a different learning rate scheduler (e.g. a fixed decrease in the learning rate). I hope that makes sense and helps.

This value is not the size of the array returned. It is how many points in the ~~array~~list are valid.

For example, if you request 1,000,000 points, the array size passed into the C function will be set to 1M samples. We will copy that many in to the list before returning. However, say it timed out, there may only be 420,042 points in the array. That is what `actual_number_of_points` means.

Seeing as PyPy doesn't guarantee cleanup until exit and in general GC behavior can vary based on [config and platform](http://doc.pypy.org/en/latest/gc_info.html), it may be good to disable `ConstructorStats` checks on PyPy. The destructor counts will likely be unreliable going from machine to machine -- no deterministic way to call `gc.collect()` the correct number of times. CPython's reference counting mostly plays well with C++'s deterministic destruction, but PyPy has a real GC which doesn't really make any cleanup promises.

Perhaps a global option can be added to pytest and guard all `ConstructorStats` checks (or replace `ConstructorStats` with a dummy class with a `==` operator which always returns true).

I finally got the time to write a minimal test script. Lacking easy access to a generic NTLM-enabled test server, the script sends a minimal payload to a MicroSoft Exchange 2007 EWS service:

```python
import requests
import requests.adapters
from requests_ntlm import HttpNtlmAuth

session = requests.Session()
session.auth = HttpNtlmAuth(username='\\john@example.com', password='mysecret', session=session)
headers = {'Content-Type': 'text/xml; charset=utf-8', 'Accept-Encoding': 'compress, gzip'}
data = b'<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?><s:Envelope xmlns:m="http://schemas.microsoft.com/exchange/services/2006/messages" xmlns:s="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/" xmlns:t="http://schemas.microsoft.com/exchange/services/2006/types"><s:Header><t:RequestServerVersion Version="Exchange2007_SP1" /></s:Header><s:Body><m:ResolveNames ReturnFullContactData="false"><m:UnresolvedEntry>DUMMY</m:UnresolvedEntry></m:ResolveNames></s:Body></s:Envelope>'

print('Pool size 2')
session.mount(prefix='https://', adapter=requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter(pool_block=True, pool_connections=2, pool_maxsize=2))
r = session.post(url='https://example.com/EWS/Exchange.asmx', headers=headers, data=data)
print(r.status_code)

print('Pool size 1')
session.mount(prefix='https://', adapter=requests.adapters.HTTPAdapter(pool_block=True, pool_connections=1, pool_maxsize=1))
r = session.post(url='https://example.com/EWS/Exchange.asmx', headers=headers, data=data)
print(r.status_code)
```

Without the patch, the test script hangs forever when the session has a max poolsize of 1. With the patch, the script completes.

Thanks for summarizing. You stated it well. Yeah, I'm familiar with the `functools.partial` approach required by `asyncio`. But it always feels unsatisfying and never natural. For example, each time you're faced with the decision of whether to pass the `*args` in the `partial()` call, or pass only the `**kwargs` and leave the `*args` for `run()`. The latter spreads the arguments across more than one invocation, which isn't ideal for code comprehension. And if the former should be used, then why not insist that `partial()` always be used? That way there'd be only "one way." (Actually, some parts of asyncio's API do require `partial`, like [`Future.add_done_callback()`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-task.html#asyncio.Future.add_done_callback).)

> That has the advantage that the class could use funcall syntax to specify the options (options=trio.RunOptions(a=1, b=2) vs. options={"a": 1, "b": 2}).

It might be worth pointing out that you can also use funcall syntax for dicts, e.g. `options=dict(a=1, b=2)`, though it wouldn't do argument name checking like a class would provide. I often do this for cases where the dict value will wind up as `kwargs` for some function.

Incidentally, if you'll indulge me, I can't help mentioning a vague, not-fully-formed idea I've had for some time, which is for Python to expose an object that could be used to encapsulate `*args` and `**kwargs` in a single object (e.g. `pkargs = Args(*args, **kwargs)`). It would be some marriage of a tuple and a dict and allow passing `*args` and `**kwargs` to a function using a single value (e.g. `func(***pkargs)` or a similar syntax). Do you know if something like this has ever been proposed?

I guess one option for marking things in conftest would be to tell people to write

```python
from pytest_trio.automark import *
```

where `pytest_trio/automark.py` contains a plugin hook that sniffs test to see if they're async, and if so then automatically applies the `trio` marker.

This is evil and magical: the idea is to exploit pytest's magic where every `conftest.py` file is automatically treated as a pytest plugin that's applied only to the tests inside the same directory as that `conftest.py`. And the effects of a pytest plugin are determined by the contents of its namespace. So if we import a hook function, then that hook function becomes part of this "conftest plugin", and is applied where-ever the conftest is in scope.

OK - thanks for the example.  Just plopping the binaries on a fileserver or standard dev or prod binary distrbution mechanism would work as well OOB from pants, but it may be the case that you or someone else uses an artifactory or nexus install for that purpose.

I'll try to explain the current state of things here more fully - in short this presents as a bug to you, but pants has not yet been designed to publish anything but library level jars, from the internal pants product scheduling pipeline to the target UIs.

In more detail, the jvm_binary single file restriction was not motivated as a restriction but as an affordance - I don't think publish even existed as a goal at that point.  The idea was jvm_binary was fundamentally just a metadata target saying this classpath over here has this main, but then its grew the ability to contribute the actual source file that carried the main to permit both simple binaries of 1 file and encourage most code to be structured (code-wise) as a library even if just to present an API to the main.  IIRC the push here was to get folks at Twitter to write mainly testable code and just have the main gather flags and positional args and instantiate and use the api.  The former being hard to test w/o shelling out, the latter being standard unit test fodder.

On to solving this.  Right now publish does not allow combination of libraries in a single publish artifact at all.  It does this to ensure in the simplest way possible that the same classfiles don't get published in 2 different jars.  So that structure and aim works against combining jars natively, dooming any attempts by Eric to help you out here at the very end.  There is though a customization mechanism, and its designed explicitly to allow attachment of additional artifacts beyond primary + sources + javadoc (+ CHANGELOG).  I think this is the way to go.  If this works well enough for you all, then it may make sense to land this in contrib so it can be a standard feature repos can turn on.

The high level docs are here: https://pantsbuild.github.io/dev_tasks_publish_extras.html
If this sounds sane to you and @ericzundel I can provide more help getting a publish plugin to attach "shaded" fatjars.

actually it's really important, there's a distinction between a request taking 'N' seconds, and a server responding w/n N seconds.

AWS for instance can immediately give you back a response stream, but then hang mid-way through reading the stream.

We have one of our services which downloads files from 10k to 800MB.  We can't say we want to request to take N seconds because it depends how big the file is.  What we can say is to fail the request if the server doesn't send any data w/in N seconds.

* Running N or N^2 queries is a bad idea when one would be enough.
* I don't see anything in the docs indicating that `QuerySelect` is not intended for use with large result sets--hence the subject of the issue.
* The WTForms implementation only runs the query once per field object, after which the results are cached. This implementation doesn't, meaning that potentially N or N^2 queries are run for *each* de/serialize call.
* It's helpful to know more about your use case, but now I'm thinking these classes mix up what should be separate concerns. One is validation, which I think would be handled better using (no surprise here) a validator. The other is loading data via ORM on deserializing, which makes more sense if written separately per ORM, and moved to a separate library. Powerline does not alter tmux window width, it only tells tmux *what* to show, not *where*. If tmux thinks that terminal is wider then it actually is (which leads to wrapping and thus that effect of “stacking” status lines) then it is tmux bug. You should get the same problem even if you set `status-left` and `status-right` options to something that has nothing to do with powerline.

Though there is another possibility: tmux determines terminal width correctly, but it is not able to correctly determine *powerline characters* width. Problem here is that some terminal emulators have equivalent to Vim’s `&ambiwidth` option: it determines whether characters with “ambiguous” east asian width property should be considered single or doublewidth. All software which does not have `&ambiwidth` option equivalent considers them single-wide and you should configure your terminal emulator same. But if that is your problem then you should get gaps like between arrow and `master` branch in the last screenshot near *all* arrows.

In any case this is not a powerline issue. You should first try to find the ambiwidth option and if terminal does not have it or it is set to “single” report to tmux bug tracker. If you are using iTerm then I should say it *does* have this option.

Hey @m2jobe,

To use scrapy-splashwith Crawlera you need to create a Lua script (an example is in crawlera docs: https://doc.scrapinghub.com/crawlera.html#using-crawlera-with-splash), and then use /execute andpoint with SplashRequest, passing this script. 

So it should be something like that (note that I combined a script from Crawlera docs and a script from scrapy-splash README in order to enable cookies, response headers, etc.):

```lua
-- myscript.lua
function main(splash)
    -- begin example from crawlera docs
    local host = "proxy.crawlera.com"
    local port = 8010
    local user = splash.args.apikey
    local password = ""
    local session_header = "X-Crawlera-Session"
    local session_id = "create"

    splash:on_request(function (request)
        request:set_header("X-Crawlera-UA", "desktop")
        request:set_header(session_header, session_id)
        request:set_proxy{host, port, username=user, password=password}
    end)

    splash:on_response_headers(function (response)
        if response.headers[session_header] ~= nil then
            session_id = response.headers[session_header]
        end
    end)
    -- end example from crawlera docs

    -- customized render script inspider by scrapy-splash examples
    splash:init_cookies(splash.args.cookies)
    assert(splash:go{
        splash.args.url,
        headers=splash.args.headers,
        http_method=splash.args.http_method,
        body=splash.args.body,
    })
    assert(splash:wait(0.5))

    local entries = splash:history()
    local last_response = entries[#entries].response
    return {
        url = splash:url(),
        headers = last_response.headers,
        http_status = last_response.status,
        cookies = splash:get_cookies(),
        html = splash:html(),
    }
end
```

Then in a spider you can load this script (or just put it into a string constant) and use SplashRequest to render it:

```py
import scrapy
from scrapy_splash import SplashRequest

with open('myscript.lua', 'rb') as f:
    lua_source = f.read().decode('utf8')


class MySpider(scrapy.Spider):
    # ...
    #def my_parse(self, response):
        # ...
        yield SplashRequest(url, self.parse_result,
            endpoint='execute',
            cache_args=['lua_source'],
            args={
                'lua_source': script, 
                'apikey': "<YOUR_API_KEY>"
            },
       )
```

Hope this helps. I haven't actually tested the code above, but I think it should work :) You can tweak the script - the basic idea is to customize an example from Crawlera docs using suggestions from scrapy-splash README.

Hello @gmetzker 

Thank you for your detailed report, I really appreciate it.

I have good news, soon `cwltool`'s treatment of inputs placed into the current working directory will  match the spec and you won't need to iterate over the array yourself: https://github.com/common-workflow-language/common-workflow-language/issues/405

You are probably still curious as to what is happening in your example above. Arrays can have multiple levels of `inputBinding`: one at the per item level, and for the overall array. Your example defines an `inputBinding` for the overall array and `cwltool` is supplying the default `inputBinding` behavior by (erroneously) including the non-current-working-directory path for each item.

Here is how to deploy your workaround until `cwltool` is brought into fuller compliance with the CWL v1.0 spec:

``` yaml
  srcFiles:
    inputBinding:
      position: 2
    type:
      type: array
      items: File
      inputBinding:
        valueFrom: $(self.basename)
```
Here I've had to use the expanded `array` syntax so that the per-item `inputBinding` can be used.

Since we already have an issue (#325) documenting `cwltool`'s need to better conform to the spec in this regard (thus eliminating the need for this workaround) I am going to close this issue. Feel free to report any other problems you have either here or by posting to https://www.biostars.org/t/cwl using the `cwl` tag.

@dset0x That's half true (or let's say incomplete): `Web_1` (w.l.o.g.) pushes to `Broker` (Redis or RabbitMQ or ...), ONE of the Workers/Consumers (`Worker_i` in the following) gets the payload from there. If `Web_1` needs the result, it gets it back. This is load balancing and you do not know who `Worker_i` will be. So you cannot update half of the workers without any additional admin work (e.g. separating queues for "old" and "new"). (feel free to ask for a proof here

As a result: If `Web_x` for any `x` wants a result sometimes (say with a probability significantly over 0), then all Workers needs to be updates in sync with all Web nodes. Or you need to split your queues, which I think is not that easy.

right. I see the problem here.  Your workflow isn't quite in line with how pipenv is designed, sorry for the confusion if this was unintuitive.  There are a few things going on:

1. We don't really intend for people to create an environment as a separate step, with no packages, and then activate the shell, step into it, and start cloning packages to install.  This is part of what we would consider an old workflow that requires you to be constantly thinking about whether you have activated a virtualenv before you can install packages, and it's something we want to discourage.  Pipenv first and foremost is a dependency manager.  Running it by itself without any dependencies to install is possible, but doesn't make a lot of sense.

2. Specifically paths in a pipenv project are relative to _the project itself_. So when you `pipenv install -e .` you are installing _the project root_, not the directory you cd into.  There are a lot of pragmatic and technical reasons for this, but mostly because if your project is not itself a package, it expects you to give it paths to packages or else the resulting Pipfile will be unusable.  Imagine that every time you install a thing, it goes into your pipfile to be reproduced later -- if we just put `-e .` in your pipfile every time you try this it wouldn't make sense given that a **project** is the basis of all pipenv operations and as such all paths are translated relative to the project root.

The workflow you'd want for something like the example you gave could be one of two things depending on your needs:

```console
$ git clone https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid.git
$ pipenv install -e ./pyramid/
```

Which would create a pipfile that looks like this:

```ini
...
[packages]
"hash" = {path = "./pyramid/", editable = true}
...
```

or you could just do the following:

```console:
$ pipenv install -e git+https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid.git#egg=pyramid
```

and that would create a pipfile like this:

```ini
...
[packages]
"pyramid" = {git = "https://github.com/Pylons/pyramid.git", editable = true}
...
```

And in the second case it wouldn't be in a local directory but would still install from VCS

As an alternative to cutting a new release, the following patch can be used to fix the test builds by preventing virtualenv from updating pip/setuptools/wheel and instead use the versions available per default on AppVeyor/Travis:

```diff
diff --git a/appveyor.yml b/appveyor.yml
index 9313a482..543ee08e 100644
--- a/appveyor.yml
+++ b/appveyor.yml
@@ -16,5 +16,6 @@ build: off
 
 test_script:
   - "python bootstrap.py"
+  - "python -m pip install --upgrade virtualenv"
   - "python -m pip install tox"
   - "tox"
diff --git a/tox.ini b/tox.ini
index 4b4b4fd8..9fd673a2 100644
--- a/tox.ini
+++ b/tox.ini
@@ -5,6 +5,8 @@
 # export TOXENV='py2{6,7},py3{3,4,5,6},pypy'
 
 [testenv]
+setenv =
+	VIRTUALENV_DOWNLOAD=0
 deps=-rtests/requirements.txt
 passenv=APPDATA USERPROFILE HOMEDRIVE HOMEPATH windir APPVEYOR
 commands=py.test {posargs}
```
This would insulate the test builds from this kind of issue happening again. Hopefully the virtualenv tests will help detecting such problems before a new release is cut.

Notice that it fails to `develop` and it fails to `install`, and the failure in `install` occurs in distutils, reinforcing the idea that `scripts`, as the namespace and documentation indicates, is for scripts and not for binary executables. Given the traceback, I suspect that even the patch in the referenced pull request would not correct the issue for installation.

It still feels wrong to me to be hacking scripts to support something which they weren't intended to support, so here are a few options to consider:

- A new feature, an option that allows installing arbitrary binary files to the `bin` or `Scripts` directories.
- Use the `data_files` to install the relevant directory (although beware of #130). 
- Store the binary files as package data and have a separate command that links or copies those files into the scripts directory following installation (a custom post-install step).

"error: could not create '/usr/lib64/python2.7/site-packages/pysam': Permission denied" means you are trying to install into a protected directory without privilege.  It's been a while since I worked with this project but if you prepend "sudo " to your command then you will request privileges for the following command.  Generally it is something you should be careful with, but this seems like a reasonable case.

You should also be able to add the option "--user" which should install the package in your user directory only for you if you prefer.

Oh interesting. Hmm, the autodiff papers always imply that mixed-mode is better for HVPs, so it would be interesting if the savings were only in overhead (or the subtle issue you mentioned that JVPs tend to be simpler than VJPs).

I agree that a clear writeup of the comparison would be super helpful, especially because I feel like I've never been able to definitively nail it down for myself. Here's some thinking-out-loud: in rev-rev mode, you have to trace over (i.e. store the intermediates of) an entire forward-backward pass on the function, so that's 2x the max memory usage of just tracing the forward pass. In fwd-rev mode, you evaluate the JVP of the VJP function, which means you only have to trace the forward pass of the function (to form the VJP), and then you can walk backward along that graph evaluating JVPs along the trace, in principle freeing up memory as you go. Doesn't that mean that the max memory usage is only as much as for `grad`?
Making the adjoint functions into primitives is a clever design idea!

Yeah that sounds like a good template for how we could bring the FLO cost down for jvp/ggnvp. I have a minor question, on the point about avoiding storing objects that aren't needed, would it actually be possible to know ahead of time that a variable isn't going to be needed by _any_ vjp?

It seems to me that if the user does something like this:
```python
def f(x):
    a = np.ones(3)
    y = np.dot(a, x)
    return y**x
```
Although the third line is linear in `x`, Autograd has no way of knowing (until the function has returned/the graph has been completed) that `x` won't be needed somewhere else during the reverse pass.

Pruning nodes surely wouldn't be able to happen until the whole graph had been constructed, by which point we don't really care, since it's the _peak_ memory usage that we wish to reduce.

Thanks for bringing this up again. It's very useful feedback and we want to fix it.

As far as I can tell, you're talking about two changes:

1. set `node.outgrads = []` after `node.sum_outgrads()` is called in `backward_pass`
2. set `node.value = 0.` after using it in `backward_pass`

Does that sound right to you?

I pushed a commit that might address these issues and also might mean you don't need to run `gc.collect()` explicitly, which can be very slow. The new commit implements change 1 above, but more importantly it addresses the circular dependency problem by adding a `preserve_tape` argument to `backward_pass` that is set to `False` for regular gradients and only `True` for `jacobian` (which now cleans up after itself when it's done with the tape). Reference counting should now be able to clean up everything.

I have a basic memory test, copied below, that runs successfully on the new commit but blew up virtual memory on the previous master. However, it might not cover your use case, so please try it out and let us know how things look.

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
from autograd import grad
na = np.newaxis

a = 10000
b = 100
c = 50

A = np.random.randn(a)
B = np.random.randn(b)
C = np.random.randn(c)

def fn(x):
    return np.sum(A[:, na, na]*B[na, :, na]*x[na, na, :])

g = grad(fn)

for i in range(50):
    g(C)
```

In this example:
IMDB dataset are user reviews classified into positive or negative review. 
First, it convert words to ids (assign a unique id to every word from the dataset), so a sentence is represented as a list of ids (integers).
Then, we embed that list for representing every id as a vector (that will learn relations between ids, this embedding will be learned during training too) so instead of a list of ids, we get a list of vectors.
Then we apply LSTM network to classify these sequences.

Hi,

ok this is a problem that we can not do much about, I will try to explain here.
We use HGNC as the definition of what genes that exists and what names they have. The problem is that HGNC is only available for human genome build 38 which we NOT use in the analysis pipeline. We map and annotate everything in human genome build 37. 
We use ensembl to find out what transcripts that belongs to a gene and what NM transcripts they have. 
So in this case if we look at the gene **SRD5A2** in ensemble build 37 we see:
```
EnsGeneID EnsTransID   Chrom Gene Start Gene End   Transcript Start   Transcript End     Gene Name    RefSeq mRNA
ENSG00000049319	ENST00000405650	2	31747550	31806136	31747550	31806136	SRD5A2
ENSG00000049319	ENST00000233139	2	31747550	31806136	31749656	31805969	SRD5A2
```
In build 38 we see

```
EnsGeneID EnsTransID   Chrom Gene Start Gene End   Transcript Start   Transcript End     Gene Name    RefSeq mRNA
ENSG00000277893	ENST00000622030	2	31522480	31581067	31522480	31581067	SRD5A2	NM_000348
```

Which means that in build 38 there is only one transcript instead of two and it now has a NM identifier.

## What to do

We can not manually map all transcripts between builds 38 and 37, that would not work anyway since tools like VEP that give information on impact (synonymous, non synonymous etc) does not know about the mapping. This means that it is potentially dangerous to use the `Clinical filter` because we will miss variants. 

Best thing would be if we could use genome build 38 all the way, at the moment it is not possible since there are too many annotations that we would loose. They are not ready for 38 yet.

Please ask questions here if anything is unclear. We need to discuss this further in a user meeting soon.

Best,

Måns

> to demonstrate that a distributed approach is valuable.

Good point. I should have numbers to back my decisions. I'll generate a comparison.

The centralized PS does spend half it's bandwidth on communicating the model, something that's not necessary with the decentralized approach. The centralized approach has approximately the same latency cost (about the same number of connections, depending on the reduce algorithm used). However, it is more resilient because only one worker is holding state.

Most existing work uses all-reduce, including [Horovod] and some distributed gradient coding papers for their experiments ([QSGD], [TernGrad]). I've seen a centralized parameter server used with [distributed Tensorflow].

[distributed Tensorflow]:https://www.tensorflow.org/deploy/distributed
[Horovod]:https://github.com/uber/horovod
[QSGD]:https://arxiv.org/abs/1610.02132
[TernGrad]:https://arxiv.org/pdf/1705.07878.pdf

Hello @GITSHOHOKU , I was able to reproduce the error with two 4.4.0-116-kernel based desktops, but then I found [this link](https://www.systutorials.com/linux-kernels/55769/revert-module-add-retpoline-tag-to-vermagic-linux-4-9-79/) that indicates that some previous decision in Linux tree was reverted and a patch has been removed from the kernel tree (see [this](https://patchwork.kernel.org/patch/10190481/) for kernel 4.4).
Apparently the change in vermagic string - `4.4.0-116-generic SMP mod_unload modversions` instead of `4.4.0-116-generic SMP mod_unload modversions retpoline`  (Note the last word removed)
did not propagate to Ubuntu distro yet, but did reach the source code tree. Hence the generated error.

I will work to establish a repro environment and update the thread when the fix is set.

In the meanwhile the alternatives are:
1 - Use librealsense [DKMS package](https://github.com/IntelRealSense/librealsense/blob/master/doc/distribution_linux.md#installing-the-packages) to install kernel patches:
>  sudo apt-get install realsense-uvcvideo
2. Switch to another supported LTS kernel, 4.10 or 4.13, and reapply the patch.

This is not an issue with librealsense, but a rather well documented missing component of Windows N installations. You will need to debug why the Media Feature Pack is not installing on your configuration. It contains the media foundation dlls which are critical to interacting with UVC camera devices as the OS layer.

Hey @thirdOctet,

This is a conundrum. On the one hand, you would never (I think) want to do what you're doing, which is to take the gradient of an edge of a gradient of a gradient of a gradient. (!!!) So in that sense it's not surprising that you're getting nonsense at the end of this thing.

On the other hand, there's various parts of that pipeline that are letting you down and maybe shouldn't. In particular,

1. due to lack of precision, the `filters.rank.gradient` call converts the float image into a uint8 image with just three levels, (0, 1, 2). I wouldn't expect anything useful to come after that!
2. the HOG image is invalid even after receiving valid(ish) input. 

@stefanv @blink1073 any thoughts on this? My instinct:

1. This is at a minimum a bug in the `img_as_ubyte` documentation, imho: it states "positive values are scaled between 0 and 255". A more accurate description would be "the interval [0.0, 1.0] is mapped to [0, 255]". Other than that, I don't see a consistent way to deal with very low dynamic range images. Perhaps we should not allow such images into rank filters?
2. This is a bug in how the HOG image is generated, and it should be rescaled to prevent this.

Thanks also here for investigating. However, I don't think there is a problem with the eigenvalue computation:

```
mat = np.random.rand(5, 5)
Hxx, Hxy, Hyy = hessian_matrix(mat, sigma=5)

H = np.zeros((5, 5, 2, 2))
H[..., 0, 0] = Hxx
H[..., 0, 1] = Hxy
H[..., 1, 0] = Hxy
H[..., 1, 1] = Hyy

l1, l2 = hessian_matrix_eigvals(Hxx, Hxy, Hyy)

for i in range(5):
    for j in range(5):
        print np.linalg.det(H[i, j] - l1[i, j] * np.eye(2))
        print np.linalg.det(H[i, j] - l2[i, j] * np.eye(2))
```

Note, that `hessian_matrix_det` only computes the approximate determinant using integral images.

The problem of the `hessian_matrix` function at the moment is, that the generated kernel (second derivative of the Gaussian) is not correct for small sigmas due to discretization issues.

It would be highly appreciated, if that kernel function would be fixed. As a start, these references should be helpful: http://campar.in.tum.de/Chair/HaukeHeibelGaussianDerivatives, http://campar.in.tum.de/twiki/pub/Chair/HaukeHeibelGaussianDerivatives/gaussian2d.m

The issue with the interface is that, with its current implementation, floating point values passed in will raise an error. After this PR they won't for `circle_perimeter`, but they still will for all other functions in the `draw` module. This is what may confuse users, I think.

I don't think Bresenham is broken. The issue with this PR is that it is selecting a sequence of non-integral points that it then rounds. There is the issue with numpy's round pointed above. My other concern is that Bresenham's algorithm [is supposed to](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Midpoint_circle_algorithm#Algorithm) select consecutive pixels where `(x-cx)**2 + (y-cy)**2 <= r**2` while maximizing `(x-cx)**2 + (y-cy)**2`, and because of the rounding I don't think this PR is doing that anymore, e.g. `circle_perimeter(0, 0.6, 3)` will select `(0, 4)`, a point for which the inequality does not hold. Now this may not be relevant if all you are after is drawing a circle that looks pretty, but I wouldn't be surprised if a skimage user was expecting more than pretty from this function.

Thanks for the report ! I was able to reproduce the issue, and also found a workaround for now. What you can do is to use a different object database implementation when instantiating the repo like so:
```python
repository = Repo('.', odbt=git.GitDB)
```

The default object database implementation changed between the versions of gitpython, from the one you see above (pure python) to one that uses a long-running git subprocess to stream objects. The latter is faster and generally preferred, but apparently interacts badly with certain signals.

As GitPython is in maintenance mode and has no functional test suite just yet (which I would need to test these interaction to find a fix), I will leave this one open hoping that others with a similar issue find it (and the workaround).

Sorry, this is a known issue: conda env build 1 tried to introduce a post-link script to add Library/bin to PATH for users, so that Matplotlib and spyder in particular could resolve their DLL dependencies.  That post-link script has an error on Python 2.7.  https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/509

The fix right now is to manually remove the .conda-env-post-link.bat script from (your install folder)\Scripts and force installation of an older build of conda-env, any time you use Conda:

```conda install scikit-image conda-env=2.4.2```

If you do not force the older version of conda env, it gets updated automatically and causes this issue again.

We are working on testing a better fix not involving the post link script, and hope to release this later today.

I hate to pile on @asmeurer, but I agree with @ukoethe.

I saw some very nasty bugs related to this problem. For instance, take the case where the package `foo` version `2.0` does not exist. If one builds all of the remaining packages ( placed here for convenience https://github.com/jakirkham/test_conda_features ), one will run into the following problem when installing `use_foo`.

    $ conda install --use-local use_foo
    Fetching package metadata: ......
    Solving package specifications: ..
    Error: Unsatisfiable package specifications.
    Generating hint: 
    [      COMPLETE      ]|###################################################| 100%


    Hint: 'use_foo' has unsatisfiable dependencies (see 'conda info use_foo')

Now, as we built this locally `conda info` won't work and `conda info --use-local use_foo` does not exist. We can try to upload these broken packages or similar to get more info. If we do that, and download/install and then remove all of these packages. We can now get info and find the following.

    $ conda info use_foo
    Fetching package metadata: ....
    Error: No packages found in current linux-64 channels matching: use_foo

This error is, of course, incorrect (and unhelpful) as we are able install `use_foo` with this `conda install track_vc11 use_foo`. However, I know this after spending time with this bug, for someone who doesn't know about this bug it will take some time to debug.

At a bare minimum, a better error message alluding to this bug and a workaround would be appreciated. However, I think the mild performance hit that @ukoethe showed is acceptable compared to these sorts of bugs.
Please update to conda 4.3 using `conda update conda`, and file a new issue if you still encounter your problem.

use

    def format_set_var(key, value):

? To keep the code succinct for cases that don't need special escaping, you could then use something like

Unfortunately, I never got around trying this out and ended up with my original solution using a custom GraphQLView. 

[You can grab the full solution and read why in my article on medium](https://medium.com/@martin.samami/make-graphene-django-and-sentry-exceptions-work-together-f796be60a901).

Here's also the code if you just want the solution:
```python
# views.py
from graphene_django.views import GraphQLView
from raven.contrib.django.raven_compat.models import client as sentry_client


class SentryGraphQLView(GraphQLView):
    def execute_graphql_request(self, *args, **kwargs):
        """Extract any exceptions and send them to Sentry"""
        result = super().execute_graphql_request(*args, **kwargs)
        if result.errors:
            for error in result.errors:
                try:
                    raise error.original_error
                except Exception:
                    sentry_client.captureException()
        return result


# urls.py
from .views import SentryGraphQLView


urlpatterns = [
    url(r'^graphql', SentryGraphQLView.as_view(schema=schema)),
]
```

I modified the `tl.layers.DenseLayer`, and add `tf.device(/cpu:0)` directly on the `tf.get_variable`. The results seems correct.
This is the modified `tl.layers.DenseLayer`:
```Python
class DenseLayer(Layer):
    """
    The :class:`DenseLayer` class is a fully connected layer.

    Parameters
    ----------
    layer : a :class:`Layer` instance
        The `Layer` class feeding into this layer.
    n_units : int
        The number of units of the layer.
    act : activation function
        The function that is applied to the layer activations.
    W_init : weights initializer
        The initializer for initializing the weight matrix.
    b_init : biases initializer or None
        The initializer for initializing the bias vector. If None, skip biases.
    W_init_args : dictionary
        The arguments for the weights tf.get_variable.
    b_init_args : dictionary
        The arguments for the biases tf.get_variable.
    name : a string or None
        An optional name to attach to this layer.

    Examples
    --------
    >>> network = tl.layers.InputLayer(x, name='input_layer')
    >>> network = tl.layers.DenseLayer(
    ...                 network,
    ...                 n_units=800,
    ...                 act = tf.nn.relu,
    ...                 W_init=tf.truncated_normal_initializer(stddev=0.1),
    ...                 name ='relu_layer'
    ...                 )

    >>> Without TensorLayer, you can do as follow.
    >>> W = tf.Variable(
    ...     tf.random_uniform([n_in, n_units], -1.0, 1.0), name='W')
    >>> b = tf.Variable(tf.zeros(shape=[n_units]), name='b')
    >>> y = tf.nn.relu(tf.matmul(inputs, W) + b)

    Notes
    -----
    If the input to this layer has more than two axes, it need to flatten the
    input by using :class:`FlattenLayer` in this case.
    """

    def __init__(
            self,
            layer=None,
            n_units=100,
            act=tf.identity,
            W_init=tf.truncated_normal_initializer(stddev=0.1),
            b_init=tf.constant_initializer(value=0.0),
            W_init_args={},
            b_init_args={},
            name='dense_layer',
    ):
        Layer.__init__(self, name=name)
        self.inputs = layer.outputs
        if self.inputs.get_shape().ndims != 2:
            raise Exception("The input dimension must be rank 2, please reshape or flatten it")

        n_in = int(self.inputs.get_shape()[-1])
        self.n_units = n_units
        print("  [TL] DenseLayer  %s: %d %s" % (self.name, self.n_units, act.__name__))
        with tf.variable_scope(name) as vs:
            with tf.device('/cpu:0'):
                W = tf.get_variable(name='W', shape=(n_in, n_units), initializer=W_init, dtype=D_TYPE, **W_init_args)
            if b_init is not None:
                try:
                    with tf.device('/cpu:0'):
                        b = tf.get_variable(name='b', shape=(n_units), initializer=b_init, dtype=D_TYPE, **b_init_args)
                except:  # If initializer is a constant, do not specify shape.
                    with tf.device('/cpu:0'):
                        b = tf.get_variable(name='b', initializer=b_init, dtype=D_TYPE, **b_init_args)
                self.outputs = act(tf.matmul(self.inputs, W) + b)
            else:
                self.outputs = act(tf.matmul(self.inputs, W))

        # Hint : list(), dict() is pass by value (shallow), without them, it is
        # pass by reference.
        self.all_layers = list(layer.all_layers)
        self.all_params = list(layer.all_params)
        self.all_drop = dict(layer.all_drop)
        self.all_layers.extend([self.outputs])
        if b_init is not None:
            self.all_params.extend([W, b])
        else:
            self.all_params.extend([W])
```

I think `pad` is the better name than `border`:

* `pad` is used in Caffe ([example](https://gist.github.com/ksimonyan/fd8800eeb36e276cd6f9#file-vgg_cnn_s_deploy-prototxt-L79))
* `padW` and `padH` seem to be used by Torch7 ([source](https://github.com/torch/nn/blob/master/SpatialConvolutionMM.lua#L16))
* For me, `pad` is clearer than `border`.

> In this way, any unused keys of `input_dict` would get propagated and be available to layers further down the chain.

I see. I was thinking of a way to implement it without touching any existing layers (like `DenseLayer`), and while the `.tag` or the `**kwargs` solution would allow this, both wouldn't allow propagating unused inputs by default.

> I can't think of why you would not want to do it this way, but maybe we're talking about different things.

We're talking of the same thing. I'm just not convinced that it's a good idea.
I see that it would be useful for side information such as the `mask` to just travel along with the main tensor, but this seems like a special case to me. For this use case, it might be cleaner to just connect all those layers to the same `InputLayer` instance providing the mask, so they've got two incoming layers (the layer below and the layer providing the mask).
It's not so useful for layers that actually produce two outputs from a single input, though, they'd all bubble up to the top (unless overwritten) and come out of `lasagne.layers.get_output()`. And a layer seeing two inputs wouldn't know if it got them from its direct incoming layer or some layer further down the chain.

In any case, your proposal (which is variant 1 in [my previous comment](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/issues/337#issuecomment-124063011)) doesn't *require* layers to propagate unused input, they could just as well do:
```python
def get_output_for(input):
    input = input['default']
    output = T.dot(self.W, input)
    return {'default': output}
```
So it would be up to the implementations of what to do. (Which can be a good thing or a bad thing.) We could of course also move this "bubbling" of unused inputs into `lasagne.helpers.get_output()`, by replacing:
```python
    all_outputs[layer] = layer.get_output_for(dict(all_outputs[layer.input_layer]), **kwargs)
```
with:
```python
    all_outputs[layer] = dict(all_outputs[layer.input_layer]) if bubble else dict()
    all_outputs[layer].update(layer.get_output_for(all_outputs[layer], **kwargs))
```
Still, this variant would require changing all existing Lasagne layer implementations. That's something we could do before the release, but I'm not convinced we should.

Adding that fix makes it pass Travis, which is nice! There is no point in reviewing this yet though (if anyone was planning on doing that), better to wait until after the imminent rebase.

> I guess it would be nice to have a shortcut for this one:
> [...]
> but maybe with a different name

Yes, that's exactly the trouble. Without a shortcut, it's completely clear what it does. And if we provide a shortcut, we need a name that a) clearly says whether it's going to affect a single layer or also the layers feeding into it and b) what exactly it's applying the penalty function to (namely, the regularizable parameters).
`penalize_network` and `penalize_layer` seems too unspecific to me, it suggests penalizing the mere existence of the network or layer. `penalize_network_weights` and `penalize_layer_weights` seems slightly wrong to Eben, because it could be applying the penalty to regularizable parameters that are not "weights". On the contrary, `penalize_network_params` and `penalize_layer_params` doesn't say that it's only affecting regularizable parameters (and we can't make `**tags` part of the signature, because we already need to support `**kwargs` passed on to the penalty function). `apply_weight_decay` sounds the most natural, but it doesn't say whether it's going to affect a single layer or a full network, and it's a misnomer once you use a different penalty function than L1 or L2 (e.g., some sparsity target, however useful that would be).

If you come up with a good name, I'm all up for a shortcut! I think names are pretty important. Bad names make any code a lot harder to work with (see `iter_train`, `iter_valid` and `iter_test` in the old MNIST example), and we don't want the framework to be hard to work with.

> to me it always made sense that a "train" function would actually be a generator that performs an epoch at each step

That's a useful pattern if the `train` function needs to be stateful. Otherwise, having a `for _ in range(num_epochs)` or a `while True` as the outer loop and calling `train` in the body may be more flexible.

> More generally, the idea is to layer complex API functions (e.g. fit()) on top of simpler, more flexible API functions (e.g. train()), so that people can choose from a number of different flexibility levels and easily switch between them. The code for the high-level functions would be pretty readable, with short functions, because they build on the lower-level API, so it would be easy to copy/paste and adapt. Those functions would basically do double duty as API functions and as examples.

This nails what I had in mind. If `fit()` does what you need, just use `fit()`, otherwise copy/paste the source code of `fit()` and adapt it. (And the same holds for the lower-level functions that `fit()` calls. If you expand everything, you would end up with what the MNIST example looks like.) This gets difficult when we start using classes, so we should try and see if they can be avoided.

Hi,

regarding these fields
```
tp_free,
tp_is_gc,
tp_bases,
tp_mro,
tp_cache,
tp_subclasses,
tp_weaklist
```
I don't see that we have to worry about them. If they are not set, python automatically sets good defaults when `PyType_Ready` is called.

For the inplace sequence operations. First of all, python double checks if they are set to `NULL`, so python does not blindly rely on the flag only. Looking through the source all access to this fields were protected via `sq_inplace_concat != NULL`.
The only drawback of the current implementation (leaving it NULL) is, if Acquistion wraps an object which has (only) this slot set. Which can only be an object defined in a C-Extension. In that case proxying does not happen.
However I don't see how to fix this easily. This has to be set on the type, so as soon as its defined all the users of it see 'it is not NULL' => looks like it is implemented so lets call it. Unfortunately on type definition we don't know what kind of object we wrap.
If we implement that on our types we must return `Py_NotImplemented` if the wrapped object does not have the slot set, however right now I cannot judge if this a valid way to go or not.
What do you think how much we have to care about this case anyway ?
It only appears if there is a type defined in a C-Extensions + implementing only this slot.
For the 'normal' cases where `__iadd__` is defined on the python level the current mechanics (falling back to the number slots) work.

`Py_TPFLAGS_HAVE_FINALIZE`, do you see a case for implementing/using it ?
The current `tp_dealloc` in Acquisition does not need 'Safe object finalization'. Leaving it NULL is fine if the flag is not set.

`tp_as_async` here I could image that we wrap these calls so that the python methods
```
__aiter__
__anext__
__await__
```
are called with a acquisition wrapped `self`.

RE the failure case being buggy: We ran into that in the early days and I banged my head on the wall for a long time trying to figure out why. It would be nice if that was a hard error instead of a subtle bug, or worse, lots of conflicts that get resolved in a very expensive way.

Spitballing here: Perhaps if get() does call newTransaction() and the transaction knows its start time, and Connection keeps track of its "consistent as-of time" (_txn_time doesn't seem to fit the bill) it could raise an error?

The issue is that you're providing a fixed value as the ``unsolved_puzzle`` default.

Your factory code is equivalent to the following code:

```python
default_unsolved_puzzle = utils.clone_list(list(EASY_PUZZLE))

def SudokuPuzzleFactory():
    unsolved_puzzle = default_unsolved_puzzle  # This is not what you intended, I guess
    solved_puzzle = utils.clone_list(unsolved_puzzle)
    return SudokuPuzzle.objects.create(
        unsolved_puzzle=unsolved_puzzle,
        solved_puzzle=solved_puzzle,
    )
```

This could be fixed by changing your factory to:

```python
class SudokuPuzzleFactory(factory.django.DjangoModelFactory):
    class Meta:
        model = models.SudokuPuzzle

    unsolved_puzzle = factory.LazyFunction(lambda: utils.clone_list(list(EASY_PUZZLE)))
    solved_puzzle = factory.LazyAttribute(
        lambda obj: utils.clone_list(obj.unsolved_puzzle))
```

That's because you don't have the right libraries currently.  You're missing `-lgomp`.  Or at least when I do the equivalent compile with my own mkl library, it gives a bunch of errors about missing `GOMP_*` and `omp_*` symbols.

When you take it upon yourself to provide explicit LINKFLAGS, it assumes you know what you're doing, so once it got compiler errors with that, it didn't try adding on '-lgomp' to the list.  So the MKL tests all failed and it just went without a BLAS library.  You should see near the top of the scons output that all the BLAS options it tried failed.

That depends.

Let's say you are putting both class A and B inside a single DEX, then the call from A to B will never involve any external classes. 
if you put class A in classes.dex and B in classes1.dex, then you are required to add both dex files to `Analysis` in order to find the references.
If only classes.dex is loaded, B will be external, if only classes1.dex is loaded then A will be external.
If you load both dex files, it's back to normal and both classes are found.

Usually all the Android API classes and the java/javax stuff is not implemented inside the dex as it is available on the system, all those classes and methods end up as external.

@hubertmis Ok, great, we've found the issue. It's a bug in Mbed TLS that has already been fixed but not yet merged: As you can see looking at [this part of `ssl_read`](https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/blob/development/library/ssl_tls.c#L6881), the code-path handling handshake when expecting application data is only included if `MBEDTLS_SSL_RENEGOTIATION` is set, and otherwise handshake messages are always treated as fatal. This is wrong - they must always be considered, and only the path which [accepts renegotiation](https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbedtls/blob/development/library/ssl_tls.c#L6966) should be conditional on `MBEDTLS_SSL_RENEGOTIATION`. I will check our internal ticket for that and see if I can push it.

@turon, you are prematurely jumping to conclusions and confusing the issue.  Let me try to address the issues directly.

> Imagine the following setup: 3 devices A, B and C. A is in reach of B, but out of reach of C and vice-versa. Create network (leader) on node B, then join A and C to B as routers. Using mesh local addresses ping can pass A->B, B->A, B->C, C->B, but not A->C or C->A. The first showstopper I found is when A wants to send a packet to unknown mesh-local adress, it sends address query to B and AddressResolver::HandleAddressQuery is executed, however no response is sent because neither the mesh-C address matches mesh-B (obvious), nor any child of B exists with mesh-C address.

Do all three of your nodes have the same Mesh Local prefix?  If so, the Address Query should propagate from A to C.  Also, when a device becomes a router

> To be more precise B->children list is empty. The reason for this is that A and C are removed from children list when A/C->MleRouter::HandleAddressSolicitResponse is called, their short address is chaged from "child-short" to "router-short", LinkRequest sent to B and B->MleRouter::HandleLinkRequest is executed (A and C are removed from B->children when handling Tlv::kSourceAddress).
Without having access to Thread specification I am unsure how this scenario should be handled. Should A and C be left in the B->children list or AddressQuery go past routers or maybe just pass through the AddressQuerry to other routers?

This is correct.  When a device becomes a router, it is no longer a child of any other router.

> P.S. It seems that multicast addresses are also not retransmitted to other routers/nodes.

@hyperknot The output of `bleach.clean()` is safe for HTML. When using the output with templating languages like Jinja2 and Django, it should be marked as safe. If you don't do that, then you've got multiple things doing sanitization passes on the text. It's much harder to reason about the safety properties and shape of the output in that scenario. It's better to have one thing sanitize the text.

Given that, I don't agree that every template language's best practices are as you state. Most/many/all of them include a `|safe` filter or annotation for text that is safe for HTML context for this purpose.

Regarding use with JS libs and frameworks, it should work. Projects that I work on have used Bleach this way. The use case in this issue is that the output of `bleach.clean()` is being used both for output in an HTML context as well as being editable. That's a complicated scenario, but I think it's more correctly solved as having the user edit the *original* text and then round-tripping that with the server to get the sanitized text for HTML context. That's more correct, but it requires a round-trip to the server and that kind of sucks.

I'd love to talk about your specific scenario and help you through it. If it's exactly the same as this issue (need to use `bleach.clean` output for a field that a user edits), we can keep the conversation here. Otherwise can you create a new issue for it?

In either case, more details about your specific problem would be helpful. Can you point me to the source code? If not, can you prepare an example that shows the issue?

Hi @KaiserKarel !

When you're multiplying `initial` by `float` value, it is converted into string [here](https://github.com/limist/py-moneyed/blob/master/moneyed/classes.py#L143) and then into Decimal. But when you're multiplying it by `Decimal` constructed from `float` it is not exactly `0.9`:

```
>>> decimal.Decimal(0.9)  # In your test
Decimal('0.90000000000000002220446049250313080847263336181640625')
>>> decimal.Decimal('0.9')  # This is a conversion result of float value `0.9` in `py-moneyed`
Decimal('0.9')
```

This behavior is documented [here](https://docs.python.org/3/library/decimal.html#decimal-objects)  (there is a paragraph that describes `Decimal` behavior when `float` is passed).

Also, there is a [warning](https://github.com/limist/py-moneyed/blob/master/moneyed/classes.py#L141), that multiplying by `float` values is deprecated and I think it should be removed to be in line with default Python behavior for such cases:

```
>>> decimal.Decimal('0.9') * 0.1
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for *: 'Decimal' and 'float'
```

But this issue should be addressed in `py-moneyed` though.

@gpcimino There is another consideration that occurs to me. In `_source_is_newer` you call `getinfo` twice. Which is reasonable, but `getinfo` may require a request for network filesystems. Two requests per file copied would likely result in a slow copy.

A custom `copy_fs` and `copy_dir` could get all the info for each directory which would be more efficient.

I concur that if there is going to be a lot of common functionality then it would be worth factoring out a private function there. 

> Using a custom walker could be a neat implementation, but from the user point of view won't be as clear as a simple function parameter like copy_if_newer.

True, but maybe the `copy_fs_if_newer` could just invoke `copy_fs(foo_fs, bar_fs, walker=NewerFilesWalker)`. Then we have a NewerFilesWalker we can use elsewhere. Just a suggestion...

Are you asking whether or not other nonrel backends will automatically work because of this patch?

That depends on how much they've overwritten. Most of the changes are in the execute_sql methods of each compiler.

Here's a quick list of changes/compatibility guide:

Public API
* Add ``self.pre_sql_setup()`` at the top of the ``execute_sql()`` method of each compiler. This should have been called in previous versions, but this is the first time it's actually necessary for nonrel stuff.
* Add ``results=None`` as a default parameter to the``results_iter()`` signature.
* aggregate imports and attributes moved/were renamed. See the new NonrelCompiler.execute_sql() method for info on how to fix issues that arise from this.
* connection.use_debug_cursor is now connection.force_debug_cursor. According to the patch's author, it should now only return a binary, not tertiary, boolean (True/False vs True/FalseNone).

Private API
* django 1.8 supports multiple converters, so calling ``query.convert_values()`` no longer works. You'll have to either learn the new ``get_converters()`` API or just use ``self.connection.ops.convert_values()``
* ``_decode_child`` fixes

I don't like snapshot testing for such assertions because it moves the expected behavior to a separate file. Any test that is setting up the scenario for testing in the test file should also contain the expected behavior.

IMO snapshot testing should be used where the test scenario setup is also coming from an external location and the test itself is not changing anything. For e.g. if the test takes in a dtc file, raw data and the expected output.

1. Yes
2. The xml in the `<resMD>` tag is not, but my code makes it playable by adding the `<res>` from the favorite.
3. - The favorite contains the information you need when browsing favorites, like given title (the one that can be changed in the official controller), a description (mouseover in the controller) and its index. It also contains the `<res>` tag.
   - The reference contains the metadata of the library item, but not the `<res>` tag. This includes the title as specified in the mp3.

**Some example XML:**

Favorite:
```XML
<item id="FV:2/53" parentID="FV:2" restricted="false">
    <dc:title>Test Favorite</dc:title>
    <upnp:class>object.itemobject.item.sonos-favorite</upnp:class>
    <r:ordinal>10</r:ordinal>
    <res protocolInfo="x-rincon-playlist:*:*:*">x-rincon-playlist:RINCON_000E5875680601400#A:ALBUMARTIST/Adele</res>
    <r:type>instantPlay</r:type>
    <r:description>Interpret</r:description>
    <r:resMD>[...]</r:resMD>
</item>
```
Reference:
```XML
<DIDL-Lite xmlns:dc="http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/" xmlns:upnp="urn:schemas-upnp-org:metadata-1-0/upnp/" xmlns:r="urn:schemas-rinconnetworks-com:metadata-1-0/" xmlns="urn:schemas-upnp-org:metadata-1-0/DIDL-Lite/">
    <item id="A:ALBUMARTIST/Adele" parentID="A:ALBUMARTIST" restricted="true">
        <dc:title>Adele</dc:title>
        <upnp:class>object.container.person.musicArtist</upnp:class>
        <desc id="cdudn" nameSpace="urn:schemas-rinconnetworks-com:metadata-1-0/">RINCON_AssociatedZPUDN</desc>
    </item>
</DIDL-Lite>
```

```XML
<container id="A:ALBUMARTIST/Adele" parentID="A:ALBUMARTIST" restricted="true">
    <dc:title>Adele</dc:title>

There is a bug in path support in python 2.6. We had some previous discussion about it here: https://github.com/SoCo/SoCo/pull/154 (You may need to view comments on outdated diffs to see it):

> This seems to be a bug in Elementtree < 1.3 (i.e. the version in Python 2.6). Your fix is too widely targeted though, since it testing for xml.etree.ElementTree' in sys.modules will be true for Python 3.

> You are right however that the .// is not in fact needed at all here, since the item element is always an immediate child of the top level element, in this particular case.

> So in fact, the best solution is for you to remove the xml_find_prefix code, and remove the ".//" in all cases where you have otherwise used it. i.e. in your patch, replace

> for item in metadata.findall(self.xml_find_prefix +
    '{urn:schemas-upnp-org:metadata-1-0/DIDL-Lite/}item'): 
with

>  for item in metadata.findall('{urn:schemas-upnp-org:metadata-1-0/DIDL-Lite/}item'): 


One way to handle it might be to find the `device` element, and then conduct a search of its immediate children (i.e. avoid ".//" as much as possible).  I haven't tested this though.

The radio stuff is a matter of legacy. I think for all cases where (some of) the returned information fits into on of the data structures for a media, then that is the preferred form. I have a PR (#328) to finally make this happen for the radio stuff.

I don't think we have every really decided on a general practice in the project, so this is just my personal view. In general I think it depends on the nature if the returned information:

 * I think, that if the information fits **naturally** in a data structure, then use or make one. See e.g. all the search and browse methods. If not then:
 * If these are all "values" for a single entity of something, then I think a dict would be a good choice.
 * The there are only a few (2-3) and 1 or 2 or these are more support information then actual information (say the number of results, success status etc.) then I would use multiple returns i.e. tuple. As a side note, I really fond of named tuples for these cases (well after I learned about them anyway).

If you can give information about what it is you are returning, then I can give you my personal view on this specific case.

The DWT is basically repeated application of convolution with a scaling or wavelet filter followed by decimation (downsampling) by two.  Here is a filterbank diagram from wikipedia illustrating this:
![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6528957/24782238/e4d85f2c-1b12-11e7-8012-80ec4984385d.png)

After a certain number of levels, the discrete signal has been decimated as far as possible (i.e. the decimated signal becomes shorter than the filter length).

Here is the actual C code for computing the maximum number of DWT levels:
https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/blob/dd7b79332f6afac345be8dae3e6dffe2edb5facc/pywt/_extensions/c/common.c#L89-L94

I'm all for making it as generic as possible and I can see now that I've looked again at your code that that was your intention. However, I think it would be good to be as consistent as possible with existing Alerta auth concepts while taking into account the specifics of SAML.

So, if `group` is not a standard SAML attribute then maybe you could make that configurable. ie. allow setting a `SAML_GROUP_ATTR` that can then be used to get an attribute to match against the list of `ALLOWED_SAML_GROUPS` to allow/deny access to Alerta.

In your case it would be `SAML_GROUP_ATTR=group` (which could be the default) and `ALLOWED_SAML_GROUPS=['alerta_rw']`.

Essentially I'm just suggesting breaking this up into two config variables ...
```
ALLOWED_SAML2_ATTRS = {
    'group': ['alerta_ro', 'alerta_rw']
}
```
... to ...
```
SAML_GROUP_ATTR='group'  # default
ALLOWED_SAML_GROUPS=['alerta_ro', 'alerta_rw']
```

Hi @mblayman. I'd like to see if I can fix the the conflict, but I'm currently not even sure anymore if the issue adressed in this PR really complies with the JSON API standard. Please give me some time as I cannot check it right away.

So I looked at possible ways to implement this. Because we obviously can't do it on the view level there are only 2 places this can be done: the serializer field responsible for relation or directly in the renderer.

The 1st approach is much cleaner, but is not passible due to `HyperlinkedIdentityField` being [inflated](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/blob/develop/rest_framework_json_api/renderers.py#L99) in the renderer (and we don't have control over that field). So this approach will be possible only if we expect/force everybody to extend from the [custom](https://github.com/django-json-api/django-rest-framework-json-api/blob/develop/rest_framework_json_api/serializers.py#L138) `ModelSerializer` meaning that shifts control of the serializer fields to the library developers and allows to implement much more on the field itself. Because if I can expect a `ResourceRelatedFeild` for every relation I can create a custom field that wraps the relation when `ResourceRelatedFeild(many=True)` is used by overriding [many_init](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/blob/6bbb2890cbc00a3736e6e8513b28754f2c51a74c/rest_framework/relations.py#L88).

Looks like it boils down to the decision on where to put the overhead, on the users having to extend from a custom serializer, or the package developers implementing pretty much all the logic in renderer..

I had the same problem two weeks ago and I couldn't find any solution that worked for me including editing android.py so I came up with this solution (not practical but it actually works):
1- Go to your project directory and select the option "Show hidden files".
2- When step 1 is done, you will see a new folder called "_.buildozer_". Then go to _.buildozer/android/platform/build/dists/APPNAME/bin_. You'll see there are some files inside including apks.
3- Copy all this files (no cut) and paste it to _.buildozer/android/platform/build/dists/APPNAME/build/outputs/apk_. You'll have to create the folders "_outputs_" and "_apk_" manually. 
4- When you get your files on the folder "apk" look for a file called "_APPNAME-version-debug.apk_" and rename it to  "_APPNAME-debug.apk_" (delete the version from the name).
5- Run the command _buildozer -v android debug_ again and the final apk will appear on your rootdirectory/bin.

I will only recommend to use this if you ran out of options like in my case because it is very tedious and you have to repeat the process every time you want to create an apk.

@wizardforcel 

你不应该将你对这个问题的思考限制在机器（或深度）学习上。

因为你的想法在一开始就没有可操作性：想要找到“越准”的特征太难了。当你找到之后，GFW只需要很短的时间就能学到新的规律，然后将这条规律加入屏蔽规则。而这一切都可以在不伤害正常服务的前提下完成（另外别忘了GFW之前几年做的实验，包括主动访问测试等等）。

而你呢？就又必须去寻找一条新的、“准确的”特征来绕过。这一切都需要时间和样本，而你无论在时间和样本采集上都赢不了。

> 如果常见协议，比如 HTTP、FTP 的特征很明显，SS 又跟他们不一样，墙至少能够识别出“这不是常见协议”，然后按照滥封无辜的尿性，还是会封。

其实如果真的是这样的话，那么最终大家只能自己去实现自己的私有代理，而不开源了。通过假装正常协议的方法可能一开始有效，但是寿命会非常短暂。

Sorry, this issue has nothing to do with `django-celery-beat`.  Just for anyone's reference who hits this issue page in future, I'll describe what solved the issue for me.  I had not replaced `django.conf:settings` in the celery.py file with the django `settings` variable.

In `proj/proj/celery.py`, this line:
```
celery_app.config_from_object('django.conf:settings', namespace='CELERY')
```

Should be,
```
celery_app.config_from_object(settings, namespace='CELERY')
```

Don't forget the`from django.conf import settings` at the top of the file.

Make sure you understand this page in the documentation:
http://python-docx.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/styles-understanding.html

Briefly, latent styles cannot be applied to a paragraph (or other object) in `python-pptx`. Only defined styles available in `document.styles` can be applied. So you either restrict yourself to styles already available in the document, or add any desired styles before attempting to use them.

See this documentation page as well:

I would try changing the content type of the document part to match a regular Word document and see if that does the trick. As far as I know, there are no actual differences between a template and a regular document, just essentially the content type acting as a flag to let Word know to behave differently with that document.

The document part is available on `Document.part`.

Content types are enumerated in `docx.opc.constants`. I think the one you want is WML_DOCUMENT_MAIN. I believe this will change the content type:

```python
from docx.opc.constants import CONTENT_TYPE as CT

document = Document('foo.dotx')
document_part = document.part
document_part._content_type = CT.WML_DOCUMENT_MAIN
```

Let us know how you go.

Say, by the way, if you change your mind on a pull request (PR), you don't have to create a new one. Just add the fix on the same branch and rebase to groom your previous change out of the branch. The PR points to your branch rather than a particular commit. Actually it points from your tip of that branch to the first shared commit. That's why only your new ones show up.

It's also best practice to create a new, dedicated branch for your PR rather than doing it on master or even develop. Maybe a name like fix/tiff-res.

This SO page has some interesting details:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/7947322/preferred-github-workflow-for-updating-a-pull-request-after-code-review

Avoids unnecessary clutter on the issues list :)

Ok, I figured it out.

1. I had to poke around in the xml source for my template file, to figure
out that the abstract numbering ID I needed was "12." So I hard-coded that
in.

2. I created a function called _reset_list() which manually adds XML
elements to the first item in my list, to reset the numbering (which I
deduced by poking around on the OpenXML spec on MSDN). I call this function
on the first iteration of my item list.

Ugly, but it works, so for the time being I'm satisfied.

(Tangentially, I used a similar manual XML manipulation technique to edit
my template's header, found in headers1.xml in the source document).

* `INCLUDE_INSTALL_DIR` is set in your [root CMakeLists.txt](https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/CMakeLists.txt#L289) (I don't think it's a standard cmake variable)
* `CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX` has a default value on each platform, but is frequently modified by the user to change the install location (for example with `cmake .. -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/usr`). This should not be hard-coded anywhere, since the user must be able to change it.
* `CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH` is a way to tell cmake where to look for software when you call `find_package`. It's analogous to the `PKG_CONFIG_PATH`.

It sounds like most of you used the `standalone` plugin to get your certs the first time, but you want a renewal option. In this case, you can use the `--pre-hook` and `--post-hook` flags to give commands to `certbot` to use during renewal. In short, the flags can be used with `standalone` to stop and then restart your webserver only when actually renewing your certificate (for a few seconds every 2-3 months). An example of this with more details is given in the [documentation](https://certbot.eff.org/docs/using.html#renewal).

While I hope this works for you, like Kuba stated above, I don't see how a webroot solution using port 443 is technically feasible. `webroot` cleverly solves the problem of having webserver specific code by utilizing the fact that most webservers serve files contained in a webroot/public_html directory. Because of this, the `webroot` plugin is able to solve the [HTTP challenge](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-02#section-7.2) by adding the necessary files to your webroot directory. Performing this challenge type over HTTPS is not allowed as stated in the spec.

The challenge type that can be performed over port 443 is the [TLS SNI challenge](https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-ietf-acme-acme-02#section-7.3). In short, the way this challenge works is by configuring your webserver to respond to an HTTPS request for a randomly generated SNI value with a self-signed certificate containing that random value (and no other random values). The random value is chosen by the Let's Encrypt server and given to the Certbot client to setup.

In order to solve this challenge, the Certbot plugin needs to be able to configure your webserver to serve specific certificates for arbitrary SNI values that are given to the client seconds before performing the challenge. This requires understanding and being able to modify your webserver's configuration, not simply dropping files somewhere in your file system. This is the approach taken by both the Apache and Nginx plugins, but I don't believe it's possible to do this for an arbitrary webserver, each of which has their own configuration language, conventions, executables, etc.

If someone thinks they have a technical suggestion on how to do this within the current confines of the ACME spec, I'd love to hear it or see a PR. In the absence of such a suggestion, I'm going to mark this issue as won't fix. If you think the spec should be changed to accommodate this, please open an issue on https://github.com/ietf-wg-acme/acme.

I hope this all makes sense. While I'd love to see such a feature, I don't think it's technically possible.

@joohoi  It might be difficult to find, one has to pull in a tree of files:

`/etc/httpd/conf/httpd.conf ` declares:

```
<IfModule mod_ssl.c>
   Include "conf/vhosts.ssl.conf"
</IfModule>
```
`/etc/httpd/conf/vhosts.ssl.conf ` declares:

`Listen 443`

However, `mod_ssl` is only activated if variable `SSL` has been declared.

Also included from `/etc/httpd/conf/httpd.conf `, via `Include "conf.modules.d/*.conf"` is file `/etc/httpd/conf.modules.d/00-ssl.conf` with this content:

```
<IfDefine SSL>
LoadModule ssl_module modules/mod_ssl.so
</IfDefine>
```
And finally, `SSL` is declared in `/etc/systemd/system/httpd.service.d/override.conf` as the Justified Ancients of Systemd demand:

```
[Service]
Environment=OPTIONS=-DSSL
```

Thanks. I was able to reproduce the problem.

You can work around the problem by changing:
```
    server_names_hash_bucket_size 64;
```
to
```
    server_names_hash_bucket_size 128;
```
in your `nginx.conf`.

If you're curious why, when authenticating your domain with Let's Encrypt Certbot temporarily creates a server block with with the server name `<64 character hash>.acme.invalid`. This long domain can cause failures according to [Nginx documentation](https://nginx.org/en/docs/http/server_names.html#optimization), so Certbot will temporarily include `server_names_hash_bucket_size 128;` in your configuration file, but only if you haven't already set the directive in yourself in your config.

Certbot should do better. Perhaps it should temporarily change the value if it's current value is less than 128? @jsha, this isn't a `boulder` problem, but if you have spare cycles I'm curious your thoughts because I know you've worked on this before.

@wmarshall484: Thanks for the detailed follow-up.  I've never looked into using `dill` on one side `cloudpickle` on the other.  Primarily because there are differences in how certain objects are serialized between both packages, and thus some objects might not translate.  Your case seems to have an easy workaround, others may not.  It should be worth investigating, however.  One other thing to note is that `pyspark` has had a number of discussions and PRs submitted by their devs to natively support `dill`.  As it stands, they still only use `cloudpickle` -- which they forked the development of from `picloud` once `picloud` went commercial. 

This may be another one of the things that `dill` should be looking at if it wants to support `cloudpickle` serialization.
Regardless, @stonebig: what's the reason for replacing `pyreadline`?  is it just that it is not being maintained going forward?
