While I don't like that we end up building our own structures for handling too much database stuff outside of frameworks (Kolibri is supposed to be database-agnostic, so there are few assumptions to be made), I think we can let us guide by solving problems for the general use case when it makes sense.

This is my conclusion of who we should target:

1. Users who run `kolibri start` from command line
1. Especially early releases of Kolibri where the kinds of problems that prompt a downgrade are essential.
1. Releases that contain data migrations: But let's try to avoid that?
1. Releases that contain other types migrations that are irreversible: We should totally prohibit that. Please let me know if there are any problems with this as a policy?

Some additional use case will remain uncovered:

1. Users of installers who have no command line interaction
1. Automated installers that upgrade Kolibri in the background (PPA distribution for instance) and then restart the service automatically

In case 1), we can argue that this user would not be able to install the database backup from command line anyways. In case 2) we can argue that the intention (extreme caution!) of the database upgrade isn't relevant, we cannot ship upgrades that need this level of caution.

Paradox of db-tracked migrations:

 * We have to run a database backup *before* initializing a database connection
 * We have to connect to the database to know if there are un-applied migrations
 * The database configuration is stored in `django.settings`

Here is a draft of what I think could be the design.

Objectives for UX:

 * Automatically prompt user during **upgrades** if database should be backed up
 * Connect to the database first, if there are migrations, recommend the user to do an upgrade and show the command for the upgrade, i.e. `kolibri backup --dest=/path/` (see mockup below)
 * Store database in a location sub-folder with timestamp in name. Do not rename files, this will make instructions for restoring more complicated.
 * Make target directory user-changable, default: `~/.kolibri/backups/` (satisfied by the command line option)
 * Do not prompt:
    * During downgrades, no migrations will be run. If someone is downgrading and they want to un-apply migrations, we need a mapping from their old installation environment to see everything that has changed. This is beyond the scope.
    * If we are on a dev environment: The upgrade target to+from are both non-stable versions.
    * When not prompting, output something like "Skipping backup process".

UX mock-up:

```
$ pip install kolibri --upgrade
$ kolibri start
INFO     Version was 0.6.dev020170830184305-git, new version: 0.6.1

Upgrade detected!

Migrations were detected for your upgrade.

We recommend that you quit now and run the following command to save your database files (size: 10 MB)

kolibri backup --dest=/home/aron/.kolibri/backups

Quit now and stop the upgrade? [Y/n]
```

Kolibri backup:

```
$ kolibri backup --dest=/home/aron/.kolibri/backups
Starting kolibri backup...

Detected database, size: 10 MB
Destination: /home/aron/.kolibri/backups

Continue with backup? [Y/n]

Files backed up in:

Destination: /home/aron/.kolibri/backups/2017-09-18-110023/

To restore, copy the files to /home/aron/.kolibri/
```

So, this happens because the API `patch` isn't made until that snackbar goes away. The idea is you can batch remove a ton of different resources and have all of that equate to a single API call (The removals stack into a single operation that you can undo). Or, if you end up hitting undo, it would be only 1 call rather than 2 (or however many items it is you removed).

The problem, I think, is that we're navigating to a page that doesn't have a `workingResources` state property before snackbar disappears. If you navigate to "add resources", this works fine, but only because `workingResources` is carried over.

I tried fixing this by passing a duplicate of the array (pass-by-value rather than pass-by-reference) to the autosave function. But I'm pretty sure that, because the snackbar's `hideCallback` isn't called until it's actually hidden (which may be on another page), it doesn't work as planned. 

Potential quick fixes _could_ be binding the callback (though that might not work, since the element will have been destroyed by this point anyway?) or passing the data needed as parameters (with defaults?) to the callback.

Long term, it'd be nice if we could leverage [`vue-router`'s In-Component guards](https://router.vuejs.org/en/advanced/navigation-guards.html#in-component-guards). I made an attempt that ultimately ended up taking too long. Main obstacle is our modified version of `vue-router` to use actions rather than state. One approach (suggested by @rtibbles) I was trying but gave up on is binding a dummy component (with state) to the route for the sole purpose of using the guard. It wasn't working for me, but by that point I thought I was in too deep a rabbit hole.

> Can you share more about your use case?
Our engineering team has a large number of projects split across a number of technologies. In order to (a) make development look more like production and (b) ease the pain of setup for all devs, we run each project in an LXC that are mounted within an Ubuntu VM. In this case, we're using `watchman` inside one of those LXCs to trigger builds to a static app.

So far in what I've described `watchman` works fine.

The problem is that not every developer wants to use `vi` or Ubuntu tools to do their editing. To support people working from their host OS, we share the files *from* Ubuntu via AFP *to* OSX. It's when I read files on OSX that I trigger rebuilds.

That's no *so* bad for reading an individual file. We use `make` or [broccoli](https://github.com/broccolijs/broccoli) to avoid doing too much re-work. It's a real problem when the editor scans all the files or when you do a project-wide search, though.

Ah, that context is helpful.  I've previously commented on acceptance into distributions [here](https://github.com/facebook/watchman/issues/30#issuecomment-39049000).  The TL;DR is that we're happy to triage and figure out how best to make that happen, but we won't necessarily bend to every request or do the work to make that happen.

With that in mind; let's figure out how to solve this problem!

Could you tell me more about what specifically is the blocker here?  The modules mentioned in the OP are now effectively internal modules that we compile as static libraries and link into our final executable.  It's not clear from skimming the bugzilla issue you reference whether the linkage is a separate issue from the bundling.

Here's some context on each of the modules mentioned in the OP:

- jansson: modified to support BSER templating, then ported to C++.  There is no effective upstream at this point, because of both of these differences.  We're going to migrate to folly at some point in the future (this is gated by reducing the boost dependencies in folly).
- wildmatch didn't exist as a standalone library, and the code has no formal upstream.
- tap: modified for our C++ environment and likely to be replaced with something that is a better C++ fit in the future
- libart: significant changes for C++.  The upstream targets C99.  I have a patch series that will replace this once I've resolved some perf issues.

What are acceptable resolutions for this, given the above?

@nswamy unfortunately I don't think it is possible to implement within Java user code (unless we leverage finalize)  -  Users cannot access java heap (in our case,  reference number of one object) - the only way  is to dump from outside.

Moreover, not necessarily to be multi-threading, even single-thread can have problem:
```scala
var a: NDArray
collector.withScope {
a = new NDArray
}  // a is disposed here
a.toArray() // fail
```
As I stated, it is users' responsibility not to leak new NDArray outside of scope, otherwise they need to use `manual()`, and dispose it later.

Every tool has its own limitation, e.g., users are not supposed to use `HashMap` in multi-threads - otherwise they need to do synchronize themselves.

As you can see, this tool make disposing super easy: https://github.com/yzhliu/mxnet/pull/5/files#diff-19b434167a8c2d81db271f8c47975ff6R43

- Examples are sometimes not end to end scripts but provide several utilities to achieve a specific task

There's a pull request #7470 meant to facilitate training of various flavours of RNN and support iterators feeding various sequence lengths. 

Current design is more adapted to text as it assumes that data feed is of dimension : seq.length X batch.size, to which embedding is applied. If you take a look at the demo of the new functionalities [here](https://github.com/jeremiedb/mxnet_R_bucketing), you can see that it would be quite simple to adapt the model graph by omitting the embedding operator and instead have an iterator that feed data of shape features.number X batch.size X sequence.length. 

By working with the mx.symbol.RNN operator, the modeling approach is essentially the same as for the feedforwardmode: just need to build the symbol graph, for which the `rnn.graph` is a helper function and then train using `mx.rnn.buckets`. Caveat: for now only GPU is supported. 

Your feedback on whether you think these functionalities would be helpful are most welcome.

@ashb I think I finally nailed it. The issue was in my understanding how sqlalchemy was dealing with timezone information inside fields for mysql. If you insert in mysql `2018-08-08 20:40:21.443732+02:00` mysql will ignore the `+02:00` _and_ apply the connection's timezone setting (e.g. `set time_zone='+01:00'`). This creates chaos obviously, although not too much as long as you do not change the connection's timezone. In my tests I was actually doing so hence they didn't pass. Strangely enough if executing them isolated / locally it worked for some reason. probably due to connection re-use. 

Anyways I borrowed you event watcher and now make sure we always connect in UTC with mysql. For postgres it doesn't matter and we can test it doesn't.
